title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about artificial intelligence and its growing role in everyday life. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Invisible Assistant
content.subtitle: An article about how artificial intelligence is changing the way we live and work.
content.text:
For most of human history, a tool was something you could hold, sharpen, or put down. Today, many of the most consequential tools we use are invisible — algorithms running on distant servers, filtering the information we see, shaping the choices presented to us, and, increasingly, making decisions on our behalf. [31] What distinguishes artificial intelligence from earlier forms of automation is not merely its speed or accuracy, but its apparent capacity to adapt — to learn from data in ways that can produce outcomes its designers did not explicitly programme. [/31]

The practical applications of AI in daily life have expanded rapidly. Recommendation systems decide which films, products, and news stories appear before our eyes. Navigation apps recalculate routes in real time based on thousands of data points. Medical imaging software can detect cancerous cells with a precision that matches — and in some cases exceeds — that of trained radiologists. [32] In each of these cases, AI is performing a task that was previously thought to require human intelligence, and in many cases performing it faster and more consistently. [/32] The question this raises is not simply what AI can do, but what it should do — and who decides.

Opponents of AI are sometimes characterised as technophobes who fail to appreciate the genuine improvements the technology delivers. This misrepresents the nature of the concern. [33] The most serious criticisms are not of AI itself but of the governance structures — or their absence — surrounding its deployment. Algorithmic systems can perpetuate and amplify existing biases if they are trained on data that reflects historical inequalities. They can obscure accountability, making it difficult to identify who is responsible when a decision causes harm. [/33]

The workplace has emerged as a site of particular anxiety. Automation has displaced workers before — most dramatically during industrialisation — and societies eventually adapted, finding new kinds of work for those whose old roles disappeared. [34] Whether this time will be different is a question that divides economists. Some argue that AI will generate new industries and roles, as previous technological waves did; others contend that AI's ability to perform cognitive as well as manual tasks represents a qualitative shift that could displace a far broader swathe of employment. [/34]

In education, the arrival of large language models capable of writing essays and solving problems has forced educators to revisit fundamental questions about what learning is for. [35] If a student can generate a passable essay in seconds, what is the value of teaching essay-writing? The most thoughtful responses suggest that the answer lies not in banning these tools, but in reorienting education towards the capacities that AI cannot replicate: critical judgement, ethical reasoning, and the ability to ask the right questions. [/35]

The trajectory of AI development is not fixed. [36] The choices made now — about transparency, about who owns data, about which applications receive investment and which are regulated — will significantly shape what kind of AI-inflected future we end up inhabiting. These are not merely technical questions; they are political and moral ones, and they deserve to be treated as such. [/36]

questions.question: What does the author identify as the defining feature of artificial intelligence compared to earlier automation?
options: A) Its ability to process information at very high speeds.
B) The fact that it operates on remote servers rather than local devices.
C) Its capacity to learn and produce outcomes beyond its original programming.
D) The invisibility of the algorithms that power it.
questions.explanation: The author says AI is distinguished by 'its apparent capacity to adapt — to learn from data in ways that can produce outcomes its designers did not explicitly programme'.

questions.question: The examples of recommendation systems and medical imaging software are used to show that AI
options: A) is now capable of replacing doctors and other medical professionals.
B) is performing tasks that previously required human cognitive ability.
C) has made entertainment and healthcare significantly cheaper.
D) is more reliable than human workers in every field where it is applied.
questions.explanation: All the examples illustrate AI 'performing a task that was previously thought to require human intelligence', not that it has replaced all human workers.

questions.question: The author argues that the most serious concerns about AI relate to
options: A) the speed at which AI is being developed without adequate testing.
B) the fear that AI will eventually develop independent consciousness.
C) a lack of regulation and the risk of built-in bias causing harm.
D) the unwillingness of technology companies to share their research.
questions.explanation: The author focuses on 'governance structures — or their absence', risks of 'perpetuating and amplifying existing biases', and 'obscuring accountability' when harm occurs.

questions.question: Economists who argue that this time AI may be 'different' believe that
options: A) AI will create just as many jobs as it destroys, as technology always has.
B) AI's cognitive abilities mean it could affect employment far more broadly than past automation.
C) the rate of technological change will give workers no time to adapt.
D) previous automation affected fewer industries than AI will.
questions.explanation: They 'contend that AI's ability to perform cognitive as well as manual tasks represents a qualitative shift that could displace a far broader swathe of employment'.

questions.question: The author implies that the most appropriate response to AI in education is to
options: A) prohibit the use of AI tools to protect the value of traditional skills.
B) redesign assessment systems so that AI cannot complete them.
C) focus on developing skills that AI is unable to replicate.
D) teach students how AI tools work so they can use them more effectively.
questions.explanation: The answer 'lies not in banning these tools, but in reorienting education towards the capacities that AI cannot replicate: critical judgement, ethical reasoning, and asking the right questions'.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the author suggests that decisions about AI development are
options: A) best left to technical experts and the companies that develop the technology.
B) already being made effectively by existing regulatory bodies.
C) not urgent since the effects of AI will only be felt in the distant future.
D) fundamentally moral and political rather than purely technical matters.
questions.explanation: The author explicitly states 'These are not merely technical questions; they are political and moral ones, and they deserve to be treated as such'.
